title: Progress in toxicogenomics to protect human health
Toxicogenomic module associations with pathogenesis: a network-based approach to understanding drug toxicity
claims: Network-based approaches using co-expression network analysis can predict drug toxicity mechanisms and phenotypes more effectively than traditional gene-level analysis.
Early network responses complement histology-based assessment in predicting long-term study outcomes.
A novel mechanism of hepatotoxicity involving endoplasmic reticulum stress and Nrf2 activation is identified.
summary: The paper introduces a novel approach using co-expression network analysis to predict drug toxicity mechanisms and phenotypes, demonstrating its effectiveness in identifying new toxicogenomic modules.

title: The human hepatocyte TXG-MAPr: gene co-expression network modules to support mechanism-based risk assessment
claims: TXG-MAPr webtool uses WGCNA from PHH TG-GATEs dataset to reveal mechanistic interpretation through gene co-expression network modules
The tool captures stress response pathways perturbed by specific stressors and shows preservation in rat systems except for DNA damage and oxidative stress responses
Module responses can be calculated from external datasets obtained with different hepatocyte cells and platforms, including targeted RNA-seq data
TXG-MAPr identifies higher basal level of intrinsic immune response in donors with pre-existing liver pathology

title: Utilizing gene co-expression networks with the rat kidney TXG-MAPr tool to enhance safety assessment, biomarker identification and human translation
claims: Gene co-expression networks can reduce complexity in gene-level transcriptomic data and provide insights into mechanisms of drug-induced adverse outcomes.
Several modules annotated for cellular stress, renal injury, and inflammation were statistically associated with concurrent renal pathologies.
Many rat kidney modules contain well-annotated, robust gene networks that are preserved across different datasets, suggesting translatability to human cases.
The TXG-MAPr tool enables the analysis of kidney gene expression data in the context of co-expression networks for safety assessment and biomarker identification.
summary: The paper introduces the rat kidney TXG-MAPr tool, which uses gene co-expression networks to enhance safety assessment and biomarker identification by analyzing complex transcriptomic data.

title: Spatio-temporal transcriptomic analysis reveals distinct nephrotoxicity, DNA damage, and regeneration response after cisplatin
claims: Cisplatin induces early DNA damage in cortical and outer-medulla proximal tubules but not glomeruli
Outer-medulla proximal tubules show sustained DNA damage response, inflammation, actin cytoskeletal remodeling, increased glycolysis, and suppression of mitochondrial activity
Later responses include cell cycle pathway activation and ribosomal biogenesis in injured OMPT regions
Whole kidney analysis is less sensitive than outer-medulla proximal tubule analysis for detecting nephrotoxicity
summary: The study uses LCM and TempO-Seq to reveal detailed spatio-temporal transcriptomic responses in rat kidney segments after cisplatin exposure, identifying OMPT as the most sensitive segment for nephrotoxicity biomarker detection.

title: Network-Based Assessment of Environmental Neurotoxicants Using High-Throughput Transcriptomics in Zebrafish Embryos.
claims: An AOP network-based framework was developed for interpreting concentration-dependent transcriptomics in zebrafish embryos related to behavioral outcomes.
Transcriptomic points of departure (tPOD) were significantly correlated with behavioral apical POD (aPOD).
Partial least-squares regression integrating neurotoxicity-relevant CRKEs and TRKEs showed the best performance for estimating aPOD.
External validation using published fish transcriptomics data supported the approach's predictive power.
